coredata.dc:title: Electrodeposited Pt nanoparticles loaded on support composites of metal-organic frameworks and carbon quantum dots for the enhancement of alcohol electrooxidation

coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks comprising divalent metal (M)-linked 4,4′-bipyridine (bpy), {[CuII 4(bpy)3(OH)2(mal)3]·4H2O} n (Cubpy) and Ni2(bpy)3(NO3)4(H2O)4(Nibpy) were modified with carbon quantum dots (Mbpy/CQD; M = Cu, Ni) to prepare a composite support. Then, platinum (Pt) nanoparticles were electrodeposited onto the modified Mbpy/CQD support for the preparation of Pt-based catalysts (Pt/Mbpy/CQD) for the electrocatalytic improvement of alcohol oxidation. The influence of Mbpy with electrodeposited Pt nanostructures on CQD support electrodes is particularly discussed in detail through numerous physical characterizations, e.g., SEM, TEM, EDX, XPS, XRD, and elemental mapping. Dispersed Pt nanoparticles with diameters of 3-10 nm can be obtained on the modified Mbpy/CQD supports with suitable chemical and structural conditions for electrocatalytic oxidation improvement. The various prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts with different compositions also enhance the kinetics of alcohol oxidation; they have a greater electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) and more development activity for alcohol oxidation than do the Pt/CQD catalysts. For the oxidation of all the selected small alcohol molecules, e.g., methanol, ethanol and isopropanol, the Pt/Cubpy/CQD catalyst composites also mostly exhibited improved catalytic activity, outstanding stability, and lower onset potential (Eonset) and CO tolerance. The favorable performance of the prepared composite catalysts is attributed to their superior ECSA owing to the suitable mixed surface of metal and functional groups of electrodeposited Pt, Mbpy, and CQD on the catalytic composites. Consequently, these as-prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD composites, which are promising oxidative heterogeneous catalysts, can be used as active and stable catalysts for alcohol oxidation in alkaline solutions.

XRD patterns of the CQD, Cubpy, Nibpy, Pt/CQD, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD catalysts.
XPS spectra of C 1s (a, d, g), N 1s (b, e, h), and O 1s (c, f, i) for Pt/CQD, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD, respectively.
FT−IR spectra of the as-prepared materials.
CVs of the prepared catalysts in 0.5 M KOH at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.
CVs and CAs of the prepared catalysts on various supporting materials in (a, b) 0.5 M methanol, (c, d) 0.5 M ethanol, and (e, f) 0.5 M iso-propanol with 0.5 M KOH at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

CO stripping voltammograms of the as-prepared catalyst in 0.5 M KOH at scan rate 50 mV s−1.
Comparison results of CV (a), CA (b), Tafel plot (c) and EIS (d) for Pt/Cubpy/CQD in methanol, ethanol and iso-propanol oxidations.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks comprising divalent metal (M)-linked 4,4′-bipyridine (bpy), {[CuII 4(bpy)3(OH)2(mal)3]·4H2O} n (Cubpy) and Ni2(bpy)3(NO3)4(H2O)4(Nibpy) were modified with carbon quantum dots (Mbpy/CQD; M = Cu, Ni) to prepare a composite support. Then, platinum (Pt) nanoparticles were electrodeposited onto the modified Mbpy/CQD support for the preparation of Pt-based catalysts (Pt/Mbpy/CQD) for the electrocatalytic improvement of alcohol oxidation. The influence of Mbpy with electrodeposited Pt nanostructures on CQD support electrodes is particularly discussed in detail through numerous physical characterizations, e.g., SEM, TEM, EDX, XPS, XRD, and elemental mapping. Dispersed Pt nanoparticles with diameters of 3-10 nm can be obtained on the modified Mbpy/CQD supports with suitable chemical and structural conditions for electrocatalytic oxidation improvement. The various prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts with different compositions also enhance the kinetics of alcohol oxidation; they have a greater electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) and more development activity for alcohol oxidation than do the Pt/CQD catalysts. For the oxidation of all the selected small alcohol molecules, e.g., methanol, ethanol and isopropanol, the Pt/Cubpy/CQD catalyst composites also mostly exhibited improved catalytic activity, outstanding stability, and lower onset potential (Eonset) and CO tolerance. The favorable performance of the prepared composite catalysts is attributed to their superior ECSA owing to the suitable mixed surface of metal and functional groups of electrodeposited Pt, Mbpy, and CQD on the catalytic composites. Consequently, these as-prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD composites, which are promising oxidative heterogeneous catalysts, can be used as active and stable catalysts for alcohol oxidation in alkaline solutions.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors declare that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the paper and its Supplementary Information files. Should any raw data files be needed in another format they are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. Source data are provided with this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric power can be produced by providing alcohol fuels straight to the anode side of a cell to convert renewable sources into electric power, which is a means of producing green energy using direct alcohol fuel cells (DAFCs) [1-3]. The thermodynamic effectiveness of a DAFC in which electric energy is directly created from alcohol fuel transformation is greater than 90% [2,3]. The presence of numerous small-molecule alcohols results in a high energy density and relief thorough decomposition [1-3]. Among various alcohols, methanol, which contains one carbon (C1) atom, is one of the most representative for fuel cells, and CO2 gas with the highest energy is produced through complete methanol oxidation. However, the use of methanol as a fuel for DAFCs is highly toxic to metallic catalyst (e.g., Pt) surfaces because of the CO intermediate produced from oxidation [1,2]. The replacement of other alcohols (such as ethanol (C2), propanol (C3), and butanol (C4)) with methanol is an option that has been studied in depth because alcohols with higher molar masses are less harmful and have higher boiling points and energy densities, providing them with stimulation for oxidation [4-9]. In addition, two-carbon (C2) alcohols, such as ethanol and ethylene glycol, and three-carbon (C3) propanol are more promising fuels. Furthermore, ethanol and isopropanol have been reported to produce less CO generation, crossover effects, and toxicity than methanol. They provide a higher energy density, but their C–C bond is not easily broken during complete oxidation [4-13]. Therefore, C2- and C3-alcohols produce various intermediates, such as carbon monoxide, aldehyde, or carboxylic species, through complete oxidation, but achieving catalyst stability is challenging.

Noble metals, e.g., Pt and Pd catalysts with complete oxidation, are assumed to be the most developed and effective catalysts for DAFCs [1,3,4,7]. In particular, Pt is generally applied, as it is the best methanol oxidation catalyst. With increasing oxidation activity and fewer intermediates, the high dispersion of Pt nanoparticles loaded on carbon materials is expected to result in exceptional electrical conductivity and a large specific surface area. Moreover, Pt-based alloy (PtM) catalysts have also been introduced to increase the performance with small amounts of Pt. The good performance of PtM alloys (M = Au, Ru, Pd, Ni, Cu) is described by the bifunctional mechanism, and the electronic effect of the PtM alloying atom [1,3,4,7,10,11,13] to create hydroxylated species on M at low potentials that favor CO2 formation from adsorbed CO on Pt.

By providing the structure and appropriate distribution of these catalytic metal nanoparticles, oxidation can be achieved with a low loading of catalyst [1-5]. A high porosity and sufficient electrical conductivity as a path for the flow of electrons from the support must be considered to provide high activity. Carbon supports with high mesopores could provide a large available surface area for catalyst dispersion and increase chemical species diffusion. A good interaction between the support and the catalytic metal nanoparticles can also potentially enhance the catalyst activity, and the support material can improve and modify the electronics of the catalyst, resulting in the enhanced reaction of the active sites on the catalyst surface and geometric effects [1-4,7,8].

By trying with other classes of carbon nanomaterials, the catalytic efficiency and stability of Pt nanoparticles could be improved by loading Pt onto a suitable support material [1,3,4,7,10,11,13]. The unique physical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQD), such as their large π-conjugated electronic structure and size-dependent quantum confinement, allow them to be applied in many approaches. CQD with ultrasmall zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials are expected to be good support materials in electrocatalysis owing to their chemical stability and excellent electrical conductivity. Modifying the surface chemistry of CQD results in a large specific surface area; therefore, it is possible to increase the catalytic activity [14,15].

A high functional surface area of carbon can be achieved by modifying it with chemicals, e.g., inorganic and organic chemicals. The coordination of new 1D, 2D, and 3D structures has been investigated for the integration of both organic and inorganic components, which could create a new dimension in catalysis development involving functional materials that have such structures and properties [1,6-8]. Bipyridine (bpy) is a compound in which two pyridine rings are linked together, and the bond between the pyridine rings in 2,2′-bpy is at the α-position of the nitrogen atom. Its chelating component can be used as a bridging ligand and has numerous applications. With respect to a stable 5-membered chelate ring upon metal coordination, a chelating donor site throughout such a connecting ligand has strong redox stability and effortless functionalization [16-19]. Based on the photocatalytic performance of metal complexes of 4,4′-bpy, Nibpy and Cubpy MOFs, are also expected to serve as catalysts for the electrocatalysis of alcohol oxidation [8,18-20]. The OHads species M (e.g., Ni and Cu) on the composite of bpy and CQD may correspondingly alter the surface electronic structure of Pt-based metal catalysts because of the strong interactions between M and Pt supporting the prepared penetrable Mbpy/CQD support.

In DAFCs with anion exchange membranes, alcohol oxidation occurs in alkaline media, and the reaction kinetics are better than those in acidic media. Therefore, the equivalent current density can increase with decreasing amounts of noble metal electrocatalysts. The bimetallic catalyst provides three important steps of the reaction, namely, alcohol dissociative chemisorption, CO poison adsorption, and CO elimination, through oxidation by adsorbed OH to generate a metal surface, which explains a number of bimetallic PtM catalyst systems [1,2,4,7,8,10,11]. Based on the above considerations, the oxidation performances of several individual alcohols (e.g., methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol) over different prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD composite electrodes in alkaline media were investigated. These studies were carried out with the aim of decreasing the use of Pt (e.g., alloys or dispersion into nanoparticles or ligands) while also determining the CO tolerance. Pt nanoparticles anchored on Mbpy/CQD were prepared by the electrodeposition of Pt on either the Mbpy or CQD support, and the oxygen-containing functional groups on Cubpy, Nibpy, and CQD were able to maintain stability in an alkaline environment. The electrodeposition method was chosen for the preparation of Pt nanoparticles because of its numerous advantages for applications in massive catalyst production. This method is economical at room temperature and involves a synthesis process with only a few steps, and a unique morphology and distribution and high metal loading of the precursor can be achieved without a stabilizer or reducing agent [1,21,22]. In addition, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), physical and chemical techniques, e.g., X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), were used to characterize our catalytic system, Pt/Mbpy/CQD (M = Cu or Ni), for improving oxidation. Electrochemical measurements (e.g., CV, CA, LSV, EIS, and CO stripping) were applied to determine the catalytic performance of these electrodes to determine the consistencies of the electrooxidation reactions. The electrochemical measurements verify that the Mbpy/CQD-supported Pt nanoparticles have exceptional catalytic activity, stability, and CO tolerance toward the oxidation of alcohols. Our studies on Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalyst systems revealed that the chemical compositions, structures, and topologies of these catalysts play vigorous roles in achieving specific reactions. As the main goal in catalyst design is to sustain the dehydrogenation ability of Pt nanoparticles while reducing CO poisoning, this effort provides an innovative approach for the preparation of metal- and chemical-functionalized CQD supports, which can be further used in comparative electrocatalysis.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized via a thermal approach with the synthesis method described in our previous article [14]. Briefly, 2.0 g of citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7. H2O, Merck) and 1.0 g of urea (CH4N2O, Ajax Finechem) were mixed and dispersed in 30 mL of DI water in a flask. Then, the homogenous solution was heated on a hot plate at 120°C until an orange–yellow solid was formed, which was washed with ethanol (C2H5OH, absolute Merck) and DI water; then, nitrogen containing carbon dots, denoted as CQD, were obtained by centrifugation.

Metal-organic framework materials of Cubpy, {[CuII 4(bipy)3(OH)2(mal)3]·4H2O} n , and Nibpy, Ni2(bpy)3(NO3)4(H2O)4, were prepared by using microwave-heating method under the identical conditions that previously reported by S. Yimklan et al. [19] The crystallinity and crystalline-phase purity was studied by powder X-ray diffraction and chemical analysis.

The catalyst supporting materials were prepared by a mechanical method. Ten percent by weight of the chemicals (e.g., Cubpy, Nibpy, and (1:1) Cubpy–Nibpy) on the CQD were used for the supporting material. The supporting material inks were prepared by mixing 2.0 mg of each catalyst supporting material in a 1.0 mL mixed solution (5%wt. Nafion + ethanol + DI water), and ultrasonication was applied for 30 min to prepare the homogenous catalyst supporting material ink. Then, 5 uL of the supporting material inks was dropped on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and let it's dry under the light lamp at ambient temperature. The loading of Pt nanoparticles on the support materials was performed by an electrodeposition method with 1 mM H2PtCl6 solution as the precursor, and cyclic voltammetry was applied with catalyst deposition cycles at potentials ranging from -0.4 to 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 2 cycles at scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The catalyst electrodes obtained were denoted as Pt/CQD, Pt/Cubpy, Pt/Nibpy, Pt/Cubpy-CQD, Pt/Nibpy-CQD, Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy-CQD. The composite materials (Pt/Mbpy/CQD) on the GCE were removed by sonication in ethanol solvent for characterization.

The crystallographic structure and composition of the selected Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts were studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, TENSOR 27, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany), X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANanalytical EMPYREAN 3, Almelo, Netherlands) with Cu-Kα radiation and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Thermo-VG Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA, Al K-Alpha). The morphologies, microstructures, and compositions of these prepared samples were surveyed through field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM IT800) and elemental mapping (high-resolution) transmission electron microscopy (TEM/HR-TEM, JEM 2100 Plus) equipped with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED).

The electrocatalytic properties of the as-prepared catalysts were measured at a PalmSens4 electrochemical workstation with a conventional three-electrode system consisting of a Pt wire as the counter electrode, a silver–silver chloride electrode (Ag/AgCl) as the reference electrode and a glassy carbon rod (GC, diameter 3 mm) coated with the prepared electrocatalysts as the working electrode. The electrolytes were solutions of 0.5 M KOH supporting electrolyte in 0.5 M CH3OH, 0.5 M C2H5OH, or 0.5 M C3H7OH for alcohol oxidation.

To combine the prominent properties of electrodeposited metal nanoparticles and functional molecules (Mbpy) onto the catalyst surface, expecting that the character of the electrodeposited metal could be controlled by the electronic state of surrounding functional species, which were reversibly converted with the simulation of an electronic field. The catalytic electrodeposition of Pt nanoparticles on the Mbpy/CQD (M= Cu or Ni) support was carried out by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in a solution of 1 mM H2PtCl6 for 2 cycles to study the active catalytic surface during alcohol oxidation (Figure SM1.). In this study, GCE was selected as the chemically inert electrode material and Mbpy/CQDs were used as the supporting material for the working electrode. The shape of the first voltammogram (black line) in the cathodic scan shows the reduction of Pt4+ ions to Pt metal, with a cathodic peak at -0.1 V, inducing the deposition of Pt nanoparticles on the surface of the Mbpy-CQD electrode (Eq. (1)). In the second voltammogram (red line), the reduction peak of Pt4+ ions shifted by < -0.1 V, further demonstrating that Pt deposition preferentially occurred on the nanoparticles produced during the first scan. Between the first and second curves, the reduction peak recorded in the second scan is almost as high as that in the first scan. This fact is consistent with the thermodynamic principle, which predicts that the growth of previously formed Pt nanoparticles is easier than the nucleation of new Pt on the GCE electrode because the deposition of Pt requires less energy. The electrodeposited Pt nanoparticles are dispersed, smooth, bright, active and stable [21,22]. Using the Faradaic peak of reduction, the graphical integration of the electrical charge from the metal electrodeposition procedure (Eq. (1)) was applied for calculating the amount of electrodeposited Pt, as shown in Eq. (2) [21-23]:
(1) PtCl6 2− + 4e−→ Pt0 + 6Cl−

The deposited Pt (mPt) mass is calculated using the following equation:

where QPt (C) is the electrical charge from the Pt deposition, 195.09 g mol−1 is the atomic weight of Pt, and the Faraday constant F = 96,485.309 C mol−1. From Eq. (2), the amount of deposited Pt and the amount of Pt loaded on different supports were determined as follows: Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD (2.76 μg cm−2) > Pt/Cubpy (2.35 μg cm−2) > Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy (2.34 μg cm-2) > Pt/Cubpy/CQD (2.04 μg cm−2) > Pt/Nibpy/CQD (1.89 μg cm−2) > Pt/CQD (1.30 μg cm−2) > Pt/Nibpy (1.19 μg cm−2). The determined amounts of Pt on the supports were slightly different depending on the chemical composition of the support. It was difficult for the Pt nanoparticles to be deposited on the CQD and Nibpy supports (less Pt was deposited); however, the mass activity (in mA mg−1 Pt) values of the prepared catalysts were compared.

The structural morphology and elemental distributions of the Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts were inspected via SEM and elemental mapping, as shown in Figure SM2. The TEM, HR-TEMand SAED results are shown in Fig. 1 . The SEM elemental mapping of the catalyst-modified electrodes confirmed the occurrence of small catalytic clusters with spherical clusters, especially for Cubpy; however, Pt, Cu, Ni, C, and O were dispersed on the catalyst surface. For Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD, the spherical clusters (∼ 50-150 nm) were composed of dispersed Pt nanoparticles (green), and some were coagulated on the supports composed of Cubpy (yellow)- and Nibpy (blue)-loaded CQDs. The red (carbon) and light blue (oxygen) dots from SEM mapping were also dispersed on the elemental map, indicating the presence of functional groups from bpy and CQD on the catalytic nanoparticles. With weak contrast, the separate Cubpy and Nibpy nanoparticles on the CQD were difficult to distinguish in the SEM images. There was a comparable coexistence of spherical Pt nanoparticles (≈ 3-5 nm) and ball-like Pt/CQD nanoclusters were 20-60 nm in size (Figure 1(a)) coagulated with spherical CQD clusters (inset Figure 1(a)) for the Pt/CQD from TEM results. The paper-like Nibpy nanosheets are shown in the inset of Figure 1(c) while spherical structure of the Cubpy nanosphere was approximately 200-500 nm (Figure 1(e)) and flower-like Cubpy clusters (Figure 1(e), (h) and (k)) are obtained.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images and SAED patterns with diffuse rings indicate the poly-nanocrystalline nature of the CQDs (Figure 1(i)) and the measured interplanar distances of the CQDs were approximately 0.33 nm, accompanying the (002) planes of graphitized carbon [13,14,21,22]. As Pt electrodeposition is the last step for catalyst preparation, Pt could possibly be an epitaxial overlayer on Mbpy-modified CQD surfaces, while the first few Pt lattice layers undergo compression because of the M−M or M−O bond distances where M = Cu or Ni in the bulk metal or metal oxide. These metal crystal structures are more likely face-centered cubic (fcc) structures, but they have dissimilar lattice parameters owing to their different atomic radii (e.g., Pt > Cu ≈ Ni), and some metal oxides are present in the composite catalysts [8,17,24,25]. In addition to the Cu component, well-resolved and interlaced fringes from HR–TEM with interplanar distances of 0.250, 0.240, and 0.210 nm are attributed to the lattice distances of the CuO(111), Cu2O(111), and Cu(111) planes, respectively, while distances of 0.230 nm and 0.190 nm related to the Pt(111) and Pt(200) planes, respectively, are obtained and expected to enhance the oxidation reaction The more flattened the Pt overlayer is, the weaker the expected binding energy of CO [13,22,23] from the addition of Pt to Mbpy modified with CQDs in our catalytic design.

The XRD pattern of the CQD (Fig. 2 ) shows a broad peak between the 2θ values of 23.89° and 40.47°, ascribed to the (002) plane of graphene and the (100) plane of the hexagonal graphitic structure of carbon, respectively, while the metals (Pt, Cu, and Ni) in the prepared nanocomposites were hardly noticeable due to the catalyst preparation.

After the CV electrodeposition of Pt onto the various supports and electrochemical measurements, the Pt/CQD, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD composites exhibit slightly different diffraction peak positions at 2θ = 39.45°, 39.67°, and 39.97°, respectively, which are attributed to the (111) planes of the Pt nanostructures The diffraction peak of Pt(111) was considerably stronger than that of all other crystallographic planes, thus confirming the favorable development of electrodeposited Pt along the (111) direction [13,22,23]. This plane is more effective at enhancing alcohol oxidation. For Cubpy catalyst, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 35.88°, 38.26°, 48.83°, and 56.38° respectively, are ascribed to the (11-1), (111), (20-2), and (021) crystallographic planes of the CuO nanostructures; the Cu2O(200) plane is obtained at 2θ = 42.53°, and the diffraction peak at 2θ = 43.70° is related to the Cu(111) plane. The diffraction peaks of Nibpy catalyst at 2θ = 25.8°, 44.72°, and 51.20° are attributed to (200), (111), and (200) crystallographic planes of Ni, while the NiO (111), (200), and (220) planes are obtained at 2θ = 37.80°, 43.3°, and 62.6°, respectively.

The grain size of electrodeposited Pt in the presence of Mbpy/CQD can be determined from the diffraction peak width by means of the Scherrer equation (DP = 0.94λ/β1/2 cosθ), where Dp is the crystallite extent, λ is the X-ray wavelength, β1/2 is the full width at half height of the diffraction peak, and θ is the Bragg angle. The calculated results (Table SM1.) show dispersed Pt nanoparticles on CQDs with an average size of approximately 3.4 nm (Pt/CQD); while the Pt nanoparticles on Cubpy or Nibpy modified on CQDs (Pt/cubpy, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, Pt/Nibpy, and Pt/Nibpy/CQD) are approximately 14 nm, those on Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD are 5.2 nm.

The chemical states of the elements in the prepared catalysts were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The wide-scan spectra of the selected Pt/CQD, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD nanocomposites mainly indicate signals for C, N, and O (Fig. 3 ) from the bpy and CQD; small peaks corresponding to the signals of the metals Pt, Cu, and Ni are observed, as shown in Figure SM3.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p clarify the chemical state of Cu (Figure SM3(d) and (e)), with distinctive peaks at approximately 934 and 955 eV, in agreement with the Cu2+ 2p 3/2 and Cu2+ 2p 1/2 energy levels, while the peaks at approximately 932 and 954 eV correspond to the Cu+ 2p 3/2 and Cu2+ 2p 1/2 energy levels, respectively. The XPS spectra of Ni 2p explain the chemical state of Ni (Figure SM3(f)), with representative peaks at 853 and 878 eV, corresponding to the Ni2+ 2p 3/2 and Ni2+ 2p 1/2 energy levels, respectively, while the peaks at 855 and 880 eV are equivalent to the Ni3+ 2p 3/2 and Ni3+ 2p 1/2 energy levels, respectively. The peaks in the Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 spectra at binding energies of 70.1 and 75.9 eV, respectively, are attributed to metallic Pt° (Figure SM3(a)-(c)). Peaks for Pt2+ species, such as PtO and Pt(OH)2, were observed at 72.9 and 76.9 eV, and those for Pt4+ species were observed at 73.8 and 78.1 eV. Given the relative intensities of the peaks, Pt mainly exists in the metallic state (Pt), and the Pt 4f XPS spectra of the Pt-Mbpy (M = Cu and Ni) nanostructures indicate that the peak shifts negatively, which indicates an electronic stage alteration of Pt when it is possibly alloyed with M.

The C 1s, O 1s and N 1s positions of the catalysts are slightly different, as shown in Table SM2-4., and the N atoms are bound by the C atoms to form equivalent bonds. The XPS spectra of the samples (Fig. 3) demonstrated similar constructive functional groups of C 1s peaks assigned to adventitious carbon species at 285 eV (C−C/C=C), 286 eV (C−O/C−N), 287 eV (C−O−C), 288 eV (C=O), and 289 eV (O−C=O/C=N). As the collected samples contained Nafion, an ink component in the catalyst preparation step, the Nafion backbone provided peaks at 290 eV (−CF−), 291 eV (−OCF2−), 292 eV (−CF2−CF2−), and 294 eV (−CF3−). The N 1s spectra are normally observed to confirm the formation of the layered bpy/CQD structure [14,18]. Multiple peaks corresponding to pyridinic-N, 399 eV (C=N−C), pyrrolic-N, 400 eV (N-H), graphitic-N, 401 eV (N−C3), and amino-N, 402 eV (C−NH2) were obtained. The XPS spectrum of O 1 s (Figure 3(c), (f) and (i)) reveals that O 1 s can be split into multiple peaks: 530 eV is ascribed to lattice oxygen and other peaks, e.g., 531 eV (O=C−O/M−OH), 532 eV (C=O/N−O), 533 eV (O−C−OH), and 535 eV (H−OH), are attributed to chemisorbed or dissociated oxygen species.

According to the process of preparing the CQD and Mbpy/CQD, nitrogen-doped CQD were expected. Incapacitating heteroatoms such as N atoms into the C lattice can expand the electrocatalytic performance [13,14,18]. The atomic radii of N atoms with higher electronegativity are similar to those of C atoms. N-doped carbon structures have attracted a great deal of attention due to their superior electron mobility and metallic character compared with undoped C atom nanostructures [4,7,13,14,18]. Therefore, N atoms with extra electrons can act as electron donors, consequently increasing the electrical conductivity of C atoms. Moreover, adding N atoms to bpy could expose the band gap by shifting the Fermi surface of the C atom, which can increase the number of active adsorption sites on the carbon surface, resulting in enhanced adsorption of metal nanoparticles on the support surface. This feature results in better electrochemical properties of the prepared catalyst, and a high-performance electrode material with better electrochemical catalytic activity is expected.

The functional groups and chemical compositions of the CQD, Cubpy, Nibpy and their mixed composites were also confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT−IR). As shown in Fig. 4 , the spectrum of the as-prepared CQD was broad at 3600-3200 cm−1, which agrees with the presence of hydroxyl (−OH) groups from carboxylic (-COOH) groups and water molecules (H−O−H) or N−H stretching of the amine. The band at 2119 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching and bending vibrations of the N=C=O group. The bands at approximately 1630 and 1561 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic stretching peaks of the C=N and N−H bonds, respectively. The band at approximately 1700 cm−1 is a typical carbonyl (C=O) group. Specifically, the band at 1400 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching and bending vibrations of C−C and C−N−C bonds. The band at 1199 cm−1 is attributed to C−O stretching, which is regularly observed for oxidized carbons. The band at 1393 cm−1 corresponds to the CH2 groups in the CQD. From the FT−IR spectra of bpy in the Cubpy and Nibpy complex, the typical peaks of bpy are at 1611 and 1450 cm−1, which can be attributed to the aromatic C−C/C=C and C−N/C−N stretching vibrations of the bipyridine ring. The peak in the range of 951 cm−1 is due to the vibration of the deformed aromatic ring of the bipyridine compound. The Mbpy/CQD (M = Cu and Ni complexes) spectra revealed new N−Cu and N−Ni bonds at approximately 640 and 480 cm−1, respectively, confirming the coupling of M2+ onto the surface of bpy and/or CQD as well as the coordination of M2+ with bipyridine [18,20,26].

The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of all the catalysts in 0.5 M KOH are shown in Fig. 5 . Similar to previous results [2,11,27], the three characteristic regions correspond to the hydrogen electrochemical reaction, double layer, and oxygen electrochemical reaction. All supports without Pt (e.g., Cubpy, Nibpy, Nibpy/CQD, and Cubpy/CQD) show no well-defined hydrogen oxidation region (Figure SM4.).

However, supports with Pt (e.g., Pt/CQD, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, Pt/Nibipy, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD) show hydrogen peaks as Pt active sites cover the modified supports. As indicated by the XPS analysis results, all catalysts with electrodeposited Pt metal on the Mbpy-modified CQD sites support the dispersion of active Pt on the prepared electrode surface, which is responsible for the increased hydrogen reaction activity of the composite electrode. Moreover, the thicknesses of the double layer regions of all the catalysts are similar.

The reduction peak (adsorption) and oxidation peak (desorption) of hydrogen at lower potentials are attributed to Pt/CQD, while the oxidation peak and reduction peak of oxygenated species at higher potentials are attributed to Pt/CQD. The small currents related to the charge and discharge of the double-layer capacity are in the double-layer region where no net electrochemical reaction occurs. The CVs of the hydrogen adsorption and desorption peaks (-0.8 to -0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) are assigned to bonded hydrogen, while broad peaks of oxidation and reduction are also obtained in the oxygen reaction peaks in the range of −0.4 to 0.0 V, the potential of the small cathodic peak matching the reduction of surface oxides.

The broad peaks for the catalysts with Cubpy (Figure SM4(a).) could be a combination of anodic and cathodic processes, as it was stated that during the forward scan, the peak was approximately -0.42 V ascribed to the Cu0/Cu+ peaks approximately -0.17 V was attributed to Cu+/Cu2+ (soluble species) due to Cu+ ⇌ Cu2+ + e−. During the backward scan, one wide reduction peak at -0.38 V was observed due to Cu2+/Cu+ (Cu2+ + e− → Cu+) reduction in 0.5 M KOH, and a very small peak approximately -0.86 V is associated with the dependence of oxygen reduction on the Cu complex structure [17,24,25]. For the CVs of catalysts with Nibpy, redox peaks attributed to Ni2+/Ni3+ interconversion are centered at potentials of approximately -0.24 and -0.38 V in the anodic and cathodic directions, respectively [8,13,25] (Figure SM4(a)). It is noteworthy that the oxidation current intensity (at -0.17 V) of the copper oxide-formed catalyst is in the following order: Cubpy > Cubpy/CQD > Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD corresponds to the Cubpy amount. These complicated peaks from Cubpy, Nibpy, and CQD are from the prepared catalysts, suggesting the synergistic effect of the various mechanisms as a reason for the conceivable creation of innovative catalytic active sites from the synergistic interaction between chemicals at the catalyst surfaces to enhance the surface's activity for the oxidation reaction.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSAH; Figure SM5.) was applied to estimate the number of electrochemically active sites on the prepared catalysts and the ECSAH value of an electrocatalyst was calculated with the following formula:

where QH denotes the total hydrogen adsorption/desorption charge (mC), 0.21 (mC cm−2) is the charge desired for hydrogen monolayer adsorption on the Pt surface, and mPt (mg cm−2) is the amount of Pt immobilized on the modified sample electrodes [13,14,21-23]. The results are as follows: the ECSAH value of Pt/Cubpy/CQD (41.0 m2 g−1) > Pt/Nibpy (40.0 m2 g−1) Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD (38.2 m2 g−1) > Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy (36.9 m2 g−1) > Pt/Cubpy (33.8 m2 g−1) > Pt/CQD (31.8 m2 g−1) > Pt/Nibpy/CQD (25.0 m2 g−1). Most of them were higher than that of Pt/CQD, except for Pt/Nibpy/CQD, as this catalyst surface might not be active for the hydrogen redox reaction (Fig. 5). The ECSAH values of the catalysts with Mbpy are greater than those of the other catalysts without Mbpy owing to their larger specific surface areas. Therefore, the Pt/Cubpy/CQD composite with the highest ECSAH is anticipated to have the highest electrocatalytic activity for oxidation.

The oxidation activities of different catalyst electrodes for various alcohols (C1, C2, and C3 for methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol, respectively) were determined from CV curves. From Figure SM6., notably, iso-propanol was representative of C3 alcohol oxidation, as the prepared catalysts were less active at the oxidation of n-propanol in this study. Fig. 6 shows the CVs of electro-oxidation in an aqueous alkaline solution of 0.5 M alcohol in 0.5 M KOH for the Pt/Mbpy/CQD electrodes in the range between −0.85 and +0.4 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Apart from small redox reactions of the chemical Mbpy (e.g., Cubpy or Nibpy)-modified CQD, no additional peaks were detected for modified Mbpy/CQD electrodes without Pt (Figure SM4(b - d). The Eonset and If of the CV peaks were acquired to quantitatively investigate the catalytic activity of oxidation. The onset potential (Eonset) and maximal potential (Ef) of the alcohol oxidation peak indicate the energy barrier of alcohol oxidation, while the peak current density (jf) signifies the maximum reaction rate where the diffusion rate and the oxidation reaction rate are combined. The current density in the backward scan (jb) indicates the oxidation of intermediates at a lower potential (Eb). All electrodes showed an amplified current density during oxidation in an alkali solution; small anodic peaks related to alcohol oxidation were detected for the Pt/CQD, Pt/Nibpy, and Pt/Nibpy/CQD catalysts, while strong anodic peaks were detected for the Pt/Cubpy/CQD, Pt/Cubpy, Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy, and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD catalysts. More details are discussed below:

For the forward scan (Figure 6(a)), the mass activity and specific activity of the prepared catalysts Pt/Cubpy, Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD, and Pt/Cubpy/CQD are rather high, and those anodic CV peaks are from the hydrogenation process (direct oxidation); however, their Ef values are slightly high (Table SM5.). The Eonset positions of the catalysts were determined at a current density of 0.05 mA cm−2. Moreover, among the catalysts, the potentials Eonset of the prepared catalysts with Nibpy are rather high, while the catalysts with Cubpy provide low Eonset values. However, the catalysts with a mix of Cubpy and Nibpy still provide a slightly lower Eonset for oxidation. This indicates that Cubpy on CQD has higher active sites on the catalyst surface and a lower barrier energy than does Nibpy on CQD for oxidation.

For the backward scan, the oxidation of the intermediate is via dehydration (called indirect oxidation). Compared with Ef, all the catalysts provide slightly lower Eb values, indicating that our catalyst surface also enhances the oxidation of intermediates. The mass activity and specific activity of backward oxidation are ordered from high to low as follows: Pt/Cubpy > Pt/Cubpy/CQD > Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD > Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy > Pt/Nibpy/CQD > Pt/CQD> Pt/Nibpy. All current densities (jb) are also less than jf, indicating less intermediate formation on the catalyst surface; in other words, direct oxidation mainly occurs on the prepared catalysts. However, different Ef and Eb values of different catalysts result from different oxidation mechanisms for fuel and intermediates. Therefore, catalysts with Cubpy result in either less intermediate oxidation or more direct oxidation.

The ratio of the forward current (jf) to the backward current (jb), the jf/jb ratio, is usually used as a simple guide to determine the ability of a Pt-based catalyst to resist CO poisoning during alcohol oxidation. From CVs (Table SM5.), the jf/jb ratios of the catalysts are mostly greater than 3.0, implying less intermediate formation on the catalyst surface. These results indicate that less CO and other intermediates formed on the catalyst surface. More CV experiments with the same catalysts were performed, and the following CV cycles of the prepared catalysts still showed less change in current density. The data are presented as the % loss, as shown in Table SM5., indicating high efficiency of intermediate tolerance of the catalysts with Cubpy.

In the forward scan (Figure 6(c)), the oxidation peak (peak jf) links chiefly to CO2 formation, where the backward oxidation peak (peak jb) is produced by other intermediates, such as carboxylic acid and aldehyde species. The jf values of Pt/Cubpy/CQD (46.4 mA cm−2) and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy (43.3 mA cm−2) are also rather high, while Pt/CQD (10.2 mA cm−2) has the lowest jf value. The oxidation performances of methanol and ethanol on our prepared catalysts were similar, as their jf values were rather comparable. Like with methanol oxidation, the Eonset of ethanol oxidation on the Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts is rather low, and the Pt/CQD show slightly high Eonset values. However, the jf/jb values (≈1) of all catalysts for ethanol oxidation are rather low compared with those for methanol oxidation, as expected, because more intermediate species (C2 atoms generate more intermediates, high jb) could be formed on the catalyst surface. Among the catalysts tested, the Pt/CQD had the lowest jf/jb values, and most of the Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts had slightly greater jf/jb values than did the other catalysts, indicating that the modification of the catalysts with Mbpy could oxidize both ethanol (direct process) and intermediates (indirect process) from the catalyst surface, although more intermediates were formed. Like methanol oxidation, the % loss of catalysts to ethanol oxidation with Cubpy is small. It is worth noting that despite the high molecular weight of alcohol, our prepared catalyst could oxidize either alcohol or its intermediates very well with comparable Eonset and Ef values.

With oxidation study of high molecular weight alcohol, the CV curves shown in Figure 6(e) were obtained for different Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts in isopropanol electrolyte. The first oxidation peak (jf1) of iso-propanol is linked mainly to the formation of CO2 and acetone, while the second oxidation peak (jf2) is attributed to the poisoning of other products e.g., carbon monoxide, acetone or iso-propanol [1,2,5,7,9]. Among the catalysts, two anodic oxidation peaks, Ef1 and Ef2, approximately -0.60 V and approximately -0.20 V, respectively, vs. Ag/AgCl are obtained for isopropanol oxidation, while their jf2 is greater than that of jf1. During the reverse scan, the metal oxides, especially M−O (e.g., M = Pt, Cu, Ni), are also reduced by approximately -0.20 V, and later, the catalyst surface is reactivated; later, the oxidation of alcohol and/or intermediates occurs at approximately -0.40 V (peak jb).

According to the results, methanol oxidation on similar catalysts has high values of Ef, indicating the difficulty of electrochemically oxidizing on Pt/Mbpy/CQD, and the currents for ethanol and iso-propanol oxidation begin to rise much more sharply at more negative potentials (Eonset), which will directly imply an improvement in fuel cell efficiency. However, the jf (j1 + j2) values of all the catalysts for iso-propanol oxidation are less specific than those for methanol and ethanol oxidation. Among the catalysts used for iso-propanol oxidation, the prepared Pt/Cubpy/CQD (8.6 mA cm−2), Pt/Cubpy (8.3 mA cm−2) and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy (10.0 mA cm−2) catalysts have high jf values, while the oxidation potentials (Ef) of these catalysts are rather similar to each other; however, their Eonset values are lower than those of the other catalysts. Compared with Pt/CQD, the addition of Mbpy to the catalysts improved the activity of the catalyst for the oxidation of higher molecular weight alcohols. However, the jf/jb ratios of catalysts for iso-propanol oxidation are diverse. Interestingly, Pt/Nibpy and Pt/Cubpy-Nibpy/CQD with low jf values show rather high jf/jb ratios, signifying either low intermediate formation or more effective elimination of the poisoning substances formed during iso-propanol electrooxidation. Like other alcohol oxidations, the % loss of catalysts with Cubpy is also small.

From the above discussion, the negative shift in Eonset (Fig. 6, Table SM5-7.) and maximal peak potential (Ef, Table SM5-7.) for the Pt/Cubpy/CQD structure substantially improved alcohol oxidation compared with that of the pure Pt/CQD structure, probably because of the synergistic effect of the highly active surface area morphology and chemical composition. The Pt/Cubpy/CQD catalyst oxidizes alcohol to CO2 more proficiently than untainted Pt/CQD and other Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts owing to the excellent tolerance of the intermediate carbonaceous species (e.g., COads and CHOads) gathered on the electrode surfaces. Different alcohols with different molecular structures and hydrogen bond donation capabilities were also found to have strong relationships with the catalytic activity. The Eonset values of Pt/Cubpy/CQD toward oxidation in alkaline media were approximately -0.56, -0.62, and -0.79 V for methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol, respectively. It should be noted that longer carbon chain alcohols start to be oxidized on Pt electrodes more effortlessly and to oxidize iso-propanol with a secondary hydroxyl group by the Pt/Cubpy/CQD catalyst even more easily [10-13].

Chronoamperometry (CA) was used to measure the stability of the catalyst (Figure 6(b), (d) and (f)) in 0.5 M KOH and alcohol during oxidation for 3600 s for at potentials of -0.25 V, -0.30 V and -0.35 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol, respectively. From the CA curves, a high catalytic activity (high jf) can be obtained in the beginning, as most of the active sites are free of adsorbed alcohols. After the reaction proceeded, the current density sharply decreased in the first period of the reaction as the double-layer capacitor formed, which is a kinetic process. As the intermediates continue to accumulate on the catalyst active sites during the oxidation process, the current gradually decreases and finally reaches a steady state. With either Nibpy or Cubpy in the catalyst system, the current density of the Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts decreased somewhat and then stabilized until 3600 s. This indicates that the Pt catalyst-based Mbpy-modified CQD composites show improved electrocatalytic activity. The Pt/CQD electrode was less stable as its current density decreased by approximately 39%, 36%, and 68% for methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol oxidation, respectively (Table SM5-7.). Alternatively, the Pt/Cubpy/CQD electrode was highly stable, as its current density decreased by approximately 0.1%, 10%, and 3% for methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol oxidation, respectively (Table SM5-7.), or the current density of Pt/Cubpy/CQD stabilization was 3, 2 and 4 times greater than the steady current density of Pt/CQD for the oxidation of methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol, respectively, at 3600 s. A higher steady current density of Pt/Cubpy/CQD indicates a stronger anti-intermediate poisoning capability during oxidation.

From incomplete alcohol electrooxidation, some Pt catalyst loss from carbonaceous residue and/or some Pt agglomeration may occur and consequently decrease the electrochemical surface area. This indicates that the composite of Mbpy and CQD provides good support for active catalyst formation and reveals a slow decrease in the oxidation activity. The excellent electrocatalytic performance of the Pt/Cubpy/CQD electrode for alcohol in alkaline solution can also be attributed to the tolerance of adsorbed CO from the synergistic effect of the bifunctional mechanism and the electronic interactions among the Pt nanoparticles and the Cubpy/CQD support. The electronic properties of Pt modified by Cubpy in the catalyst which might deviate in its geometry or atomic arrangement, affecting the electronic Pt structure (from the TEM and XPS results).

The electrode polarization curves at a scanning rate of 0.001 V s−1 were constructed from linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and the LSV curves for catalysts in different alcohol oxidations are shown in Fig. 7 . The kinetic parameters, including the exchange current density and Tafel slope, were determined from the electrode polarization curves. Excellent catalysts should have large exchange current densities (i) and small overpotentials (η) and Tafel slopes (b). The Tafel slopes were determined in the kinetic region of ca. -0.70 to -0.40 V, and most Tafel plots of the prepared catalysts fit straight lines with comparable slopes less than 120 mV dec−1 (Table SM8.). Eq. (4) is related to η, where a and b represent the overpotential, the Tafel intercept, and the Tafel slope, respectively.
(4)
η = a + b
log
i

The charge transfer coefficient (α) represents the charge transfer (b= 2.3 RT/αF) of the catalyst surface toward alcohol oxidation. The linear region of the Tafel plots was found to be in the low potential range for all catalysts as shown in Fig. 7. It should be noted that the potential range varies slightly in the electrocatalysts due to their different response to potential during LSV as they have different onset potential. The Tafel slope of catalysts was in small differences from each other, suggesting that there is a similar rate limiting step for the prepared catalysts. In the other word, all prepared catalysts with Mbpy showed similar slopes, indicating identical kinetic behaviors in the same electrolytes. For methanol, ethanol and isopropanol, the slope values are in the range of ca.148, 139 and 90 mV dec−1 as listed in Table SM8., and it is possibly related to the first electron transfer of hydrogen desorption from C-H scission in alcohol being the rate-determining step. The similarity between all the catalysts suggests that the reaction mechanism is alike. The calculated exchange current densities (i) of all prepared catalysts are listed in Table SM8. The maximum exchange current density of the Pt on Mbpy and Mbpy/CQD catalyst in methanol ethanol and isopropanol are 0.030, 0.029 and 0.037, respectively, implied a faster electrode reaction, which was a significant contribution to the improvement of the AOR activity of the catalyst. In addition, the charge transfer coefficients (α) of Mbpy and Mbpy-modified CQDs loaded with Pt indicate that the spherical clustered nanostructures can enhance the electron transfer during the AOR process and contribute to the improved AOR performance. This indicates that the Mbpy MOF and Mbpy MOF-modified CQD-supported Pt catalysts have enhanced AOR activity in alkaline media. Apart from Pt/CQD, the Tafel slopes on various Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts were comparable, confirming that a similar mechanism of alcohol oxidation is realized at 120 mV dec−1 [14,21,22]. Notably, the Tafel slopes are lower for methanol ≈ ethanol > iso-propanol. This indicates complex dehydrogenation kinetics on sensitive catalyst surfaces. Pt/CQD has the highest Tafel slope, while Pt/Cubpy/CQD has a rather small Tafel slope for all oxidations. For the three alcohol oxidations, the charge transfer coefficient of the Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalyst was greater than that of the Pt/CQD electrode, suggesting that the integration of Pt into a composite Mbpy and CQD catalyst enhanced the electrocatalytic activity throughout alcohol oxidation.

To evaluate the mechanism of oxidation of different alcohols on different catalysts, Nyquist plots of the catalysts for alcohol oxidation were generated at potentials of -0.25, -0.30, and -0.35 V for methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. Rct is directly associated with the arc diameter. The values of the parameters in the fitted equivalent circuit are shown in Fig. 8 , and the values are listed in Table SM9. A small Rct indicates a fast charge transfer rate. The faster kinetics results from the specific active crystal structure of Pt on Mbpy/CQD in the catalyst system. The Rct values of Pt/CQD, Pt/Nibpy, and Pt/Nibpy/CQD are high compared with those of other catalysts, which are expected to be inefficient for oxidation. Among the catalysts, Pt/Cubpy/CQD shows a rather low Rct for all oxidations.

Consequently, the Pt/Cubpy/CQD are proven to have a faster charge transfer rate through methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol oxidation, and the semicircle in the Nyquist plot flips over to the second quadrant for ethanol and isopropanol oxidation. This implies that the resistance, Rct, becomes negative and indicates that the rate-determining step changes from ethanol and isopropanol dehydrogenation to the oxidation of intermediates.

The effect of the catalyst on CO oxidation was evaluated by CO stripping voltammetry in 0.5 M KOH, as shown in Fig. 9 . The first cycle indicates that CO oxidation occurred on the catalyst surface, while for the Pt-based catalyst, no peak attributed to hydrogen desorption appeared. Compared with that of Pt/CQD, the oxidized CO peaks of Pt/Mbpy/CQD are negatively shifted (low Eonset,CO and Emax,CO) and high Imax,CO, revealing that the electrocatalyst can easily oxidize CO [6,7,14,21,22]. Throughout the second cycle, CO oxidation was less on Pt/Mbpy/CQD and the current density of hydrogen desorption increased, indicating that the active surface area of the catalyst was restored and ready for the upcoming alcohol oxidation. Among the catalysts studied, the negative shift in the Eonset,CO of Pt/Mbpy/CQD indicates the ease of CO intermediate oxidation; moreover, the Imax, CO for CO oxidation on all Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts is greater than that on Pt/CQD, and the Pt/Cubpy/CQD provides the highest Imax,CO (Table SM10.). The presence of adding either Nibpy or Cubpy in the catalyst system could weaken CO adsorption to Pt, thereby reducing the poisoning effect of CO while the mix of Nibpy and Cubpy supports less CO oxidation [1-6]. XPS also confirmed that negative charge transfer occurs from the Cubpy/CQD and Nibpy/CQD supports to the Pt catalyst, as indicated by the negative shift in the Pt 4f binding energy.

With smaller catalyst nanoparticles (from XRD and TEM), the OH and COad species on the small catalyst nanoparticles can migrate quickly on the particles, resulting in frequent collisions between COad and OHad species, leading to more efficient CO electrooxidation [1,5,14,21-23,28]. This suggests that Cubpy or Nibpy can also help to increase the amount of OHads near the active sites, encouraging additional oxidation of CO or intermediates and improving the number of Pt sites available for CO poisoning. Therefore, the improved activity of the 3D porous structure, increased number of available active sites, increased electronic conductivity, and active OHad species contributed to the exceptional catalytic activity of the Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts.

Moreover, the excellent performance of appropriate 2 cycles of electrodepositing Pt on Mbpy/CQD can be explained by the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups in the CQD, which increases the ECSA of the catalyst electrode, thus contributing to the increased electrochemical surface area of the composite during alcohol oxidation. With the outstanding utilization of 3D catalysts, which support the effortless diffusion of alcohol to Pt active sites for catalytic activity, the developed quantity of Pt0(111) catalysts is typically less prone to corrosion.

The most desirable catalytic features for improving Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalytic alcohol oxidation are synergistic effects, electronic effects, defect structures, and surface modifications. These effects might be combined effects that would account for the inferior catalytic ability. Moreover, the CQD are monodispersed, spherical, and homogenously distributed, providing highly active activity. The use of small CQD as a support prevented metal aggregation during catalyst preparation, resulting in a product containing small catalyst nanoparticles that were stabilized during the oxidation process. The layered catalyst nanostructured form of the composite materials, in combination with their stable active site dispersions, simplified the species access and the fast product removal from the catalyst surface. Therefore, among the prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts (Fig. 10 ) for the oxidation of diverse alcohols, Pt/Cubpy/CQD, which has high alcohol and intermediate oxidation and a low Eonset, has a small Rct, small Tafel slope, high exchange current density, and high charge transfer and thus shows superior catalytic performance.

Mbpy/CQD (M = Cu or Ni) were prepared as a composite support, and then, electrodeposition of Pt on the various Mbpy/CQD supports was performed for catalyst preparation. Additionally, the oxidation performances of several small molecular weight alcohols over Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalyst electrodes in alkaline media were studied. A discrete epitaxial layer of Pt was formed by using 2 cycles of electrodeposition. A number of characterization methods, including TEM, EDX, XPS, XRD, and elemental mapping, confirmed the presence of active dispersed metal nanoparticles (5-20 nm) on the Mbpy-functionalized CQD support surface. The electrochemical analysis results of our prepared catalysts for the oxidation of various alcohols, e.g., methanol, ethanol and iso-propanol, in alkaline media are promising. Compared with Pt/CQD, the prepared Pt/Mbpy/CQD catalysts provide a lower onset potential and higher current density with less intermediate formation. The high activity of the Pt/Mbpy/CQD composite having high electrochemical surface area is because of the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups in Mbpy/CQD. Pt/Cubpy/CQD is an improved catalyst for the selective oxidation of methanol, ethanol, and iso-propanol, and it also displays a small Tafel slope, large exchange current density, charge transfer coefficient, and long-term stability, which designates improved electrocatalytic activity. Therefore, the synergistic effect on active electrodeposited Pt metal nanoparticles on Mbpy-modified CQD via a bifunctional mechanism and electronic interactions improves the electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of alcohol and intermediates in alkaline solution, which is promising for DAFC applications.
